channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Manchester Games Network launches to put UK region 'back on the games map'
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 14, 2026

4 Min Read

A group of industry veterans have launched Manchester Games Network (MGN) to support and advocate for local developers operating within Greater Manchester.

It's notable news for developers and professionals operating in the UK region. MGN intends to consolidate Greater Manchester's position as a leading cluster for national and global game development and set out its stall during a launch event hosted at the offices of Star Citizen developer Cloud Imperium Games, which has an studio in the heart of Manchester.

MGN board member Laura Harper—a partner in the Digital, Commerce & Creative team of leading UK law firm Lewis Silkin LLP—told Game Developer the organization intends to nourish the regional games industry by providing all manner of strategic support, including networking opportunities and wider advocacy. 

"We'll advocate by acting as a voice for regional studios to not only help raise their profile but also to influence policy around support, funding, incentives and infrastructure for the region and by promoting inclusive representation by working with other regional stakeholders including the region's academic institutions," she explained. 

Related:Update: Microsoft Israel's general manager set to leave amid alleged unethical use of Azure

Fellow board member Louise Andrew—head of co-development studio d3t—explained MGN intends to create a "hub for all things game development" in the short term by encouraging local developers to connect and share knowledge. "We will be working alongside all the other local game development hubs like Format, Liv Game Network and Games Republic to form a strong voice for games in the north of the UK and we will work with UKIE and TIGA to join up with the game dev network nationally," explained Andrew. 

In the medium term, MGN hopes to build stronger ties with university students and course leaders to bolster game-focused educational pathways. "We want school students to want to stay in the region when they are considering studying games courses and for graduates from degree courses to want to stay in the region post graduating because there is strong visibility of a thriving game development industry in Manchester," added Andrew. 

The MGN launch event // Image via MGN

MGN is also keen to work with local council institutions to ensure schools and colleges know how to support younger students keen to break into the video game industry. Looking further ahead, MGN wants to raise the profile of the Greater Manchester games industry to ensure funding is made more accessible to local developers and studios. 

"We want to have a stronger voice and presence in the city, so that people in local government or people who know about funding opportunities know where to go to reach the Manchester game dev community," Andrew continued. "We would like to have a presence on the local authority website and for games to have as high a profile as all the other creative industries like TV, music, theatre and art on local government agendas. 

Related:Time Games' opening lineup includes a fantasy prediction market game

In addition, MGN wants to attract large companies such as EA, Ubisoft, and Google to the local area by working with local government. The group also stressed the importance of enabling pathways for local startups by making office space and business infrastructure affordable and accessible. 

PR veteran Stephen Hey, MGN board member and co-founder, believes Greater Manchester simply needs to rediscover its voice to establish itself as a major video game hub and views the region as something of a sleeping giant. 

"Today, Epic are still here, as are EA (with their Playdemic studio) and TT Games, who are just about to release what may well be one of the biggest entertainment products of the year with LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight. Team17 is in Salford, Cloud Imperium's 600+ studio is in the heart of the city, and we have many smaller studios punching well above their weight, outsourcing giants like d3t and smaller teams like Nequinox and Huey Games. Plus a whole ecosystem with musicians, publishers, influencer agencies—everything an international games hub needs," he explained.

Related:Last Flag dev Night Street Games is laying off staff

"The talent never went away, but we certainly lost the share of voice we should have as a games city. [...] Today is when we start to claim our place back, not just in Manchester or the UK but internationally. We have the talent, the pipeline, the ecosystem and a genuinely new energy and ambition to put Manchester back on the games map. 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/manchester-games-network-launches-to-put-the-region-back-on-the-games-map-
